content.text: is a client-side Anthropic-schema tool in Claude's Code & Execution Tools suite that enables Claude to view, create, and edit text files using precise commands like

the file operations
- You return a
with the output back to Claude
This differs from server tools like
: Make exact string replacements without rewriting entire files
: Fix, improve, and debug code by directly editing files
: Edit text documents, configuration files, and data files
: Avoid loading entire files into context for small edits
Read file contents with line numbers or list directory
Replace exact string match in file (supports multiple replacements & regex)
Create new file with specified content
Insert text after a specific line number
Undo last edit to a specific file
: Older tool versions aren't guaranteed backward compatible—always use the version matching your model.

User prompt → Claude → tool_use block with command (view/str_replace/create/etc.)
                  ↓
Your app executes file operation on filesystem
                  ↓
Returns: tool_result with file contents or operation result
                  ↓
Claude continues and responds with final answer
The tool maintains state across multiple operations in the same conversation.

# Claude requests:
{
  "command": "str_replace",
  "path": "main.py",
  "old_str": "def hello():\n    print('Hello')",
  "new_str": "def hello():\n    print('Hello, World!')"
}

# Your app executes:
with open("main.py", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()

content = content.replace(old_str, new_str)

with open("main.py", "w") as f:
    f.write(content)

# Return result

# Claude requests:
{
  "command": "insert",
  "path": "main.py",
  "line_number": 5,
  "new_str": "def helper():\n    return 'helper'\n"
}

# Your app executes:
with open("main.py", "r") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

lines.insert(5, "def helper():\n    return 'helper'\n")

with open("main.py", "w") as f:
    f.writelines(lines)

# Claude requests:
{
  "command": "delete",
  "path": "main.py",
  "start_line": 10,
  "end_line": 15
}

# Your app executes:
with open("main.py", "r") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

del lines[10:15]

with open("main.py", "w") as f:
    f.writelines(lines)

# Claude requests:
{
  "command": "undo_edit",
  "path": "main.py"
}

# Your app executes (requires git wrapper recommended):
# Option 1: Use git to revert last commit
# Option 2: Store file history and restore previous version
returns file content with line numbers (adds tokens)
Large file contents add significant tokens
Use git wrapper to commit changes automatically
: Run a git wrapper that commits changes after each edit for version control and easy undo capability.
view, str_replace, create, insert, delete, undo_edit
Text Editor vs. Code Execution tool
The Text Editor tool was released in
When working with text editor tool, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with text editor tool. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of text editor tool scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.

str_replace in Depth: Uniqueness and Edge Cases

The str_replace command is the workhorse of the text editor tool, and its uniqueness requirement is the most common source of errors. str_replace fails if old_str appears zero times (no match) or more than once (ambiguous). Understanding this upfront shapes how you use the tool effectively.

When old_str is not unique, the right fix is to extend it to include more surrounding context until it becomes unique. Never try to work around the ambiguity by making old_str shorter—this makes the problem worse. A good strategy is to include the full line containing the change plus one or two surrounding lines as context in old_str, even if you only intend to change part of that text.

Zero-match failures typically happen when old_str was transcribed incorrectly from the view output: wrong whitespace, a missing character, or a Unicode encoding difference. To debug, use view with line numbers to confirm the exact text, copy it verbatim, and pass it as old_str.

The view command supports line-range arguments for navigating large files efficiently. Without line ranges, viewing a 3000-line file consumes thousands of tokens just to find the section you want to edit. Developing a systematic navigation strategy—first view the file structure at low resolution, then zoom into the relevant range—is essential for token efficiency.

A good navigation pattern: start with view on the whole file only if it is small (under 100 lines). For larger files, view a small range at the start to find top-level structure (imports, class definitions, function names). Once you identify the region of interest, view that range specifically. Then make edits within that range.
Use view with view_range=[1, 50] to read just the file header and identify section structure
Search for a known function name or class name in the view output to identify which line range contains it
When editing a function, view from the function signature line to 20 lines past the end to capture full context
After an edit, you do not need to re-view the file to confirm—str_replace returns success or error; only view if you need to verify the result visually
For files over 500 lines, prefer inserting after a known anchor line over str_replace for additions—it avoids the need to find unique surrounding context

When Claude uses the text editor tool in production systems—not just local development—the security implications are significant. The tool can read arbitrary files and overwrite them. In agentic pipelines where Claude might be processing user-supplied paths or editing files in a shared codebase, three categories of risk require mitigation.

Path traversal is the first risk: if the path parameter can be influenced by user input, an attacker might supply "../../../etc/passwd" or similar. Mitigate by validating every path against an allowlist of permitted directories before passing it to the tool execution layer. Never pass user-supplied paths directly.

Accidental overwrites are the second risk: the create command unconditionally overwrites existing files. In a CI/CD context, this could destroy important configuration. Mitigate by treating create as a destructive action requiring validation, and by running the agent against a copy of the codebase rather than the live repository.
Run code-editing agents in a Git branch, never directly on main; use git diff after the session to review all changes before merging
Restrict the allowed path prefix to a specific project directory; reject paths outside it at the tool execution layer
Implement a dry-run mode during development: log proposed edits without applying them, then review the log before re-running in apply mode
Back up files before any agentic editing session in high-stakes environments
Set a maximum number of tool calls per agent session to prevent runaway edits in case of goal misalignment
Use undo_edit defensively: if an edit produces a test failure, the agent can undo and try a different approach without manual intervention
Text Editor Tool vs Alternative Approaches

Claude also supports bash command execution and code execution tools, and the choice between them for file editing has practical implications. The text editor tool is purpose-built for code editing with a clear undo mechanism; bash sed/awk commands are more powerful but have no undo and are harder for the model to reason about. The code execution tool runs code in a sandbox and is better for experimentation than for persistent file changes.
Use the text editor tool when the goal is to make targeted, reviewable changes to specific files in a codebase
Use bash only when the task genuinely requires shell operations (running tests, installing dependencies, git commands) that the text editor cannot handle
Do not use bash for file edits when the text editor tool is available; sed/awk error messages are harder for the model to interpret than the structured errors the text editor tool returns
The text editor tool's undo_edit operates per-session; it is lost when the session ends, so treat it as a development convenience, not a persistent undo history
In fully automated pipelines, prefer the text editor tool over bash file operations because its structured output makes success/failure parsing reliable
